stages.summary: Converted the topic into a central mystery and episode promise.
Loaded a seeded Money dossier with claims, sources, concepts, and controversies.
Compiled dossier claims into documentary scenes and simulation blueprints.
Checked claim-source-scene linkage and flagged source verification as the next gate.

dossier.central_mystery: Why do paper, metal, and database entries command real labor, power, and belief?
dossier.one_sentence_thesis: Money works because societies continually convert trust into shared accounting systems.
key_questions: What problem does money solve better than barter?
Why do debt, states, and banks matter as much as coins?
What changes when money becomes software?

timeline.label: The barter puzzle
timeline.description: A simple market shows why direct trade can fail.

timeline.label: Debt and ledgers
timeline.description: Accounts, obligations, and credit make exchange possible without immediate barter.

timeline.label: Modern bank money
timeline.description: Banks expand money-like deposits through lending and trust.

timeline.label: Protocol money
timeline.description: Crypto shifts the trust question toward code, networks, and governance.

core_concepts.label: Unit of account
core_concepts.explanation: A shared measuring stick for prices, debts, and economic memory.

core_concepts.label: Liquidity
core_concepts.explanation: How quickly an asset can become spendable without a painful discount.

core_concepts.label: Expectations
core_concepts.explanation: Beliefs about future prices can change behavior now.

misconceptions: Money is just valuable because the material is valuable.
Banks only lend out cash that savers already deposited.
Inflation has one simple cause in every context.
controversies: Barter-first origin story versus credit/debt-first accounts.
How much state power versus private exchange explains money's rise.
Whether protocol trust can outperform institutional trust.

claim_ledger.text: Money is a social technology for storing value, moving value through time, and coordinating trust among strangers.

evidence.summary: IMF Back to Basics explains money through store-of-value, unit-of-account, and medium-of-exchange functions.
evidence.quote: money can be anything that can serve as a
evidence.locator: IMF F&D Back to Basics, opening definition

supporting_evidence: Institutional explanations commonly define money through store-of-value, medium-of-exchange, and unit-of-account functions.

claim_ledger.text: The simple story that money naturally emerged from barter is useful as a teaching device but disputed as a universal history.

evidence.summary: IMF presents barter as a teaching model for why money helps indirect exchange.
evidence.locator: IMF F&D Back to Basics, barter section

evidence.summary: Manual packet lane: Graeber is used for the debt/credit critique of simple barter-origin stories.

supporting_evidence: Textbook explanations often begin with barter, while anthropological critiques emphasize debt, credit, and obligation.
counterpoints: Barter can still appear in specific settings, especially where money is scarce or institutions break down.

claim_ledger.text: States, taxation, military logistics, and legal authority have often helped standardize and enforce monetary systems.

evidence.summary: Manual packet lane: this interpretive claim needs page-level book evidence before public release.

supporting_evidence: The episode treats state power as one major driver, not the only origin story.
counterpoints: Private credit, commodity money, and local exchange networks can also generate monetary practices.

claim_ledger.text: In modern banking systems, commercial banks create deposits when they issue loans; lending is not simply relending existing cash.

evidence.summary: Bank of England explains that commercial-bank lending creates deposits in modern money systems.
evidence.quote: commercial banks create money, in the form of bank deposits
evidence.locator: Bank of England Quarterly Bulletin 2014 Q1

supporting_evidence: Central bank educational material explicitly describes bank lending as creating deposits.

claim_ledger.text: A bank run is a trust and liquidity crisis: withdrawals can outpace liquid reserves even when a bank owns longer-term assets.

evidence.summary: Bank of England material distinguishes bank deposits, central-bank money, and liquidity constraints.
evidence.locator: Bank of England Quarterly Bulletin 2014 Q1

supporting_evidence: The simulation separates liquid reserves from longer-term assets so the timing problem becomes visible.

claim_ledger.text: Inflation is better taught as a system involving money supply, goods supply, spending velocity, shocks, and expectations.

evidence.summary: Federal Reserve material defines money supply concepts used as one input in the educational inflation model.

supporting_evidence: The episode uses a simplified slider model to show interacting pressures rather than one-cause explanations.
counterpoints: Different schools of economics weight monetary, supply-side, and expectation channels differently.

claim_ledger.text: Crypto reframes the money question from institutional trust to protocol trust, but it does not eliminate trust altogether.

evidence.summary: The Bitcoin whitepaper frames the system as peer-to-peer electronic cash, supporting the protocol-trust scene.

supporting_evidence: The white paper proposes a peer-to-peer payment system, while users still rely on software, governance, exchanges, wallets, and social consensus.
counterpoints: Crypto advocates and skeptics disagree over whether protocol trust is a stronger or weaker foundation than institutional trust.

model_positions.position: Use barter as the opening intuition because users recognize the coordination problem.
Do not imply barter is the established historical origin; show debt and obligation early.
Turn the disagreement into a playable myth-vs-reality sequence.

disagreements.summary: The episode starts with barter as a puzzle, then immediately complicates it.

model_positions.position: Prevent the slider from implying a single universal inflation formula.
Use the lab as a mental model, not a prediction engine.

disagreements.summary: The scene labels the simulation as educational and keeps uncertainty visible.

visual_opportunities: A dark museum-like wall of price tags becoming a ledger.
A market map where failed barter links light up red.
A bank balance sheet that drains as confidence falls.
An inflation control board with linked sliders and visible tradeoffs.

episode.title: Money: The Shared Fiction That Runs the World
episode.subtitle: A playable documentary about trust, ledgers, banks, inflation, and protocol money.
episode.central_mystery: Why do paper, metal, and database entries command real labor, power, and belief?

scenes.title: The Shared Fiction
scenes.learning_goal: Make money feel strange before explaining it.
scenes.hook: A number on a screen can move a ship, hire a surgeon, or start a war.
narration: Why do paper, metal, and database entries command real labor, power, and belief?
The episode follows money as a trust machine: symbol, ledger, institution, and protocol.
scenes.visual_direction: Full-bleed macro shots of coins, receipts, bank ledgers, payment terminals, and database rows cutting into a glowing account balance.

scenes.title: The Barter Trap
scenes.learning_goal: Show why direct exchange breaks when wants do not align.
scenes.hook: You have fish. You need shoes. The shoemaker wants grain.
narration: Barter is a useful puzzle, but it is not the whole origin story.
The real lesson is coordination: money makes indirect exchange easier.
scenes.visual_direction: A network of traders where successful matches glow and failed wants fade.

interaction.title: Find a trade path

variables.label: Wants overlap
variables.explanation: How often two traders want what the other has.

variables.label: Trust
variables.explanation: How willing traders are to accept delayed or indirect value.

outputs.label: Trade success
outputs.explanation: Barter works when wants overlap, trust is high, and the market is large enough to find a counterparty.

interaction.learning_reveal: Money does not merely replace barter; it expands who can trade with whom.
interaction.renderer_hint: Use a force-directed exchange graph with pathfinding feedback.

scenes.title: Before Coins, There Were Ledgers
scenes.learning_goal: Introduce credit, debt, and accounting as monetary foundations.
scenes.hook: A debt written down can move value before a coin ever changes hands.
narration: Money is also memory: who owes what, to whom, and under whose authority.
This is why the barter-first story becomes more interesting when debt enters the room.
scenes.visual_direction: Clay-tablet inspired entries transforming into modern database rows.

scenes.title: Taxes, Armies, and Standard Coins
scenes.learning_goal: Show how authority can standardize monetary systems.
scenes.hook: A state can make money matter by demanding it back as taxes.
narration: Markets matter, but so do laws, taxes, wages, armies, and courts.
Money becomes more powerful when institutions make it the official language of obligation.
scenes.visual_direction: A horizontal timeline connecting minting, taxation, payroll, and legal tender.

scenes.title: The Money Banks Create
scenes.learning_goal: Make deposit creation legible without pretending banks are magic.
scenes.hook: A loan can create a deposit before anyone prints new cash.
narration: Modern money is partly created through bank balance sheets.
That makes trust, regulation, and liquidity part of the monetary machine.
scenes.visual_direction: Split balance-sheet animation: loan asset appears as deposit liability.

scenes.title: How Trust Breaks Fast
scenes.learning_goal: Let users feel the speed of a liquidity crisis.
scenes.hook: The danger is not only how much money leaves. It is how fast.
narration: A bank run compresses trust failure into hours or days.
Liquid reserves buy time; panic spends it.
scenes.visual_direction: A reserve tank draining into withdrawal streams while long-term assets stay locked.

interaction.title: Reserve runway

variables.label: Reserve ratio
variables.explanation: Liquid reserves as a share of deposits.

variables.label: Daily withdrawals
variables.explanation: Share of deposits customers try to withdraw each day.

outputs.label: Reserve runway
outputs.explanation: A bank run is a speed problem: even a bank with assets can fail if liquid reserves leave faster than assets can be sold or financed.

interaction.learning_reveal: Liquidity is time. Trust decides how quickly time disappears.
interaction.renderer_hint: Use a balance-sheet panel beside an animated reserve gauge.

scenes.title: Inflation Is a System
scenes.learning_goal: Replace one-cause inflation stories with a multi-variable mental model.
scenes.hook: Move one slider and prices twitch. Move four and the system starts talking.
narration: This lab is not a forecast; it is a map of pressures.
Money, goods, velocity, shocks, and expectations push on prices together.
scenes.visual_direction: A control room with four sliders feeding a pressure gauge and price index trail.

interaction.title: Price pressure board

variables.label: Money growth
variables.explanation: How quickly the money supply expands.

variables.label: Goods growth
variables.explanation: How quickly goods and services supply expands.

variables.label: Expectations
variables.explanation: How strongly people expect prices to rise.

outputs.label: Inflation pressure
outputs.explanation: The model keeps the key lesson visible: prices respond to money, goods, spending speed, and expectations together.

interaction.learning_reveal: Inflation debates are often fights over which pressure deserves the most weight.
interaction.renderer_hint: Use linked sliders, a gauge, and a small uncertainty band.

scenes.title: Why Smart People Disagree
scenes.learning_goal: Make disagreement legible instead of smoothing it away.
scenes.hook: The history of money is partly a fight over which story gets to be called obvious.
narration: Wonderlab does not hide interpretive conflict.
This room separates factual disputes, framing disputes, and emphasis disputes.
scenes.visual_direction: Three debate lanes for source scout, research critic, and synthesis director, with the resolved episode choice highlighted at the bottom.

scenes.title: Protocol Trust
scenes.learning_goal: Frame crypto as a trust tradeoff, not trustlessness.
scenes.hook: What if the institution is replaced by code, but the code still needs people?
narration: Crypto changes who or what users trust.
The debate is whether that substitution solves more problems than it creates.
scenes.visual_direction: Two-column debate chamber: institutional trust versus protocol trust.

scenes.title: Show Me the Evidence
scenes.learning_goal: Expose claims, confidence, and sources without derailing the story.
scenes.hook: Every glowing claim has wiring behind the wall.
narration: The source lens lets users inspect what the episode asserts, what supports it, and what remains disputed.
scenes.visual_direction: Claim cards connected to source cards and controversy markers.

scenes.title: What Money Really Buys
scenes.learning_goal: Ask the user to synthesize money as trust, memory, power, and protocol.
scenes.hook: Design a new money. What do people have to believe for it to work?
narration: Money works because societies continually convert trust into shared accounting systems.
The final challenge asks users to choose which trust failures their money system can survive.
scenes.visual_direction: A final design board where the user's choices light up risks and tradeoffs.
